root_cause: No production fault existed. The p99 latency page for the `web` service was a false alarm — either an already-recovered transient blip or a misconfigured/too-sensitive alert threshold. Direct measurement showed the service is fully healthy: over 100 samples against the real endpoint (/orders), latency was p50 ~1.9ms, p90 ~2.4ms, p99 ~4.4ms, max ~5.6ms, with 100% HTTP 200 responses. /healthz returned 'ok', the load generator reported all requests succeeding, and web had 0 restarts running on the stable release stable-1.4.2. The root `/` path returns 404, but that is expected (it is not a defined route) and is unrelated to latency.
actions_taken: Investigated only; made no changes. Ran `docker ps`/`docker stats` (all containers up, CPU/mem near idle), read the docker-compose config, tailed logs for web/load/payments/operator (no errors), probed the service directly: /orders (200, ~2ms), /healthz ('ok'), and captured a 100-request latency distribution (p99 ~4.4ms). Confirmed via `docker inspect` that web/load/payments had restarts=0 and were running steadily. Concluded the alert was spurious and deliberately took NO remediation — no restarts, rollbacks, scaling, or config edits — since any change would risk a real outage with no fault to fix. The unrelated payments neighbor was left untouched.
postmortem: Blameless summary: An on-call latency monitor paged for the `web` p99 SLO, but investigation found the service healthy the entire time (p99 ~4.4ms, all 200s, no errors, no restarts). This was a false alarm, most likely a brief self-recovered blip or an overly-sensitive/misconfigured alert threshold. The correct and least-risky response was to change nothing after verifying health, which is what was done. No customer impact occurred. The chief risk in this class of incident is a reflexive 'fix' (restart/rollback) that introduces a real outage where none existed. Follow-ups to reduce future noise: (1) review the p99 alert threshold and evaluation window (add a for-duration / multi-window burn-rate condition so single-scrape spikes do not page); (2) ensure the alert links directly to the latency dashboard and a synthetic probe so responders can confirm current state in seconds; (3) capture the firing query and sampled values in the page so an already-recovered blip is obvious at a glance. No blame attaches to anyone — the monitor did its job of surfacing a signal; we tuned our interpretation and will tune the threshold.
